text.title: Trello Butler Automation and Power-Ups
text.summary: Butler rules, board buttons, essential Power-Ups, and Trello REST API patterns for automation.

section.title: Butler Automation

rule.statement: Use Butler rules for mechanical card moves; use Butler buttons for triggered multi-step workflows that require human initiation (Start Work, Submit for Review, Archive Completed).
rule.rationale: Rules run automatically and should be reserved for deterministic transitions; buttons give humans control over multi-step state changes that should not run without intent.

example.note: Rule: "When all checklists on a card are completed → move card to Done." Button: "Start Work → move to In Development + add me + set due date 3 working days."
Using Butler to auto-move cards to Done based on due date passing — this archives incomplete work silently.

section.reference: Butler automation rule and button examples

rule.statement: Enable these Power-Ups in order of value: Butler (automation), Custom Fields (metadata), Card Aging (stale detection), Calendar (due date visualization). Add GitHub and Slack only if the team actively uses both.
rule.rationale: Free and Standard plans allow 1 Power-Up on free tier; prioritize automation and metadata over visualization. Card Aging surfaces stale work passively without a separate process.

rule.statement: Always request customFieldItems=true when fetching cards that need custom field values; resolve member IDs to names via GET /1/members/{id} before producing human-readable reports.
rule.rationale: The default card response omits custom field values; member IDs are UUIDs that agents must resolve to names for any report a human will read.

rule.statement: Archive cards via PUT /1/cards/{id}?closed=true; never delete cards.
rule.rationale: Archived cards remain queryable via GET /boards/{id}/cards?filter=closed for history and metrics; deleted cards are unrecoverable.

antipattern.description: Registering webhooks per card instead of per board.
antipattern.reason: Per-card webhooks must be re-registered whenever a card is copied or the board is recreated; board-level webhooks catch all card events in one stream.
